q: A backend team keeps a Postgres connection string and a staging API token in their project's CLAUDE.md so Claude 'always has the credentials it needs' when writing integration code. During onboarding a new hire clones the repo and the secrets are now on their laptop. A senior engineer flags this in review. What is the correct understanding of CLAUDE.md here?

options.A: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded, version-controlled context, so it must never hold secrets; reference credentials by name and inject them at runtime
options.B: CLAUDE.md is encrypted at rest by Claude Code, so storing the token there stays safe as long as the repository remains private and access-controlled to the team
options.C: Secrets in CLAUDE.md load only when a slash command explicitly pulls them in, so the new hire was never actually exposed to the values
options.D: Moving the secrets into a settings.local.json block embedded in CLAUDE.md's frontmatter keeps them out of context while still loading them at runtime

explanation: Correct: CLAUDE.md is persistent project context auto-loaded each session and is a normal version-controlled file shared on clone/pull — it is not for secrets; reference credentials by name and supply them via env vars or a secret manager, moving any extra justification out of the file itself. B is wrong: there is no special encryption that makes committing secrets safe; a private repo still distributes the file to everyone with access. C is wrong: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded, not gated behind a slash command, so the secret was loaded and shared. D is wrong: CLAUDE.md has no 'settings.local.json frontmatter,' and that is not a secret-hiding mechanism.

q: A data-engineering team wants Claude Code to run `ruff format` on every Python file the moment it is edited, with zero exceptions, even when the developer forgets to ask. One engineer added a line to CLAUDE.md saying 'Always run ruff format after editing any .py file.' Files still occasionally land unformatted. What is the reliable fix?

options.A: Bold the CLAUDE.md instruction and pin it to the very top of the file so the model consistently prioritizes it over other guidance
options.B: Add a PostToolUse hook in settings.json running `ruff format`; the harness executes it deterministically whether or not the model remembers
options.C: Create a project `/format` slash command wrapping `ruff format` and instruct everyone on the data-engineering team to remember to invoke it manually after each individual file edit they make
options.D: Switch the session into plan mode so Claude always plans and includes the formatting step before performing each file edit

explanation: Correct: deterministic 'always do X after Y' automation is a hook (PostToolUse) in settings.json — the harness runs it, so it cannot be forgotten, and any rationale belongs in docs, not the option. A is wrong: CLAUDE.md relies on the model choosing to comply, which is not reliable for zero-exception guarantees, regardless of formatting or placement. C is wrong: a slash command still depends on a human (or model) remembering to invoke it. D is wrong: plan mode is for planning complex multi-file changes, not for guaranteeing a post-edit action runs every time.

q: A platform team has built a `/deploy-preview` slash command that spins up a review environment. They put it in `.claude/commands/deploy-preview.md` and committed it. A contractor clones the repo and immediately sees the command available, but a personal `/scratch` command one developer made in `~/.claude/commands/` is nowhere to be found for the contractor. Why?

options.A: `.claude/commands/` is project-scoped and version-controlled so it ships on clone, while `~/.claude/commands/` is personal to one user/machine and is never shared
options.B: The contractor must run `claude --sync-commands` to fetch personal commands that other developers created in their own home directories
options.C: Both directories hold personal commands; the team command appeared only because the contractor happened to copy that developer's home folder during setup
options.D: `.claude/commands/` is loaded exclusively inside CI pipelines via a runner-only bootstrap step, so the contractor saw the deploy command solely because they happened to clone onto a CI runner

explanation: Correct: project commands in `.claude/commands/` are version-controlled and shared via clone/pull, while `~/.claude/commands/` is personal to a user/machine and not distributed. B is wrong: there is no command-syncing flag that pulls other developers' personal commands. C is wrong: `.claude/commands/` is genuinely project-scoped and shared, not personal. D is wrong: project commands are available in normal interactive use, not gated to CI runners.

q: A monorepo enforces that any file under `services/payments/**` must use the internal `Money` value type instead of raw floats, and that GraphQL resolvers under `**/*.resolver.ts` must include an `@auth` directive. The team wants Claude to apply these conventions automatically based on which file it touches, without anyone restating the rules each session. Which mechanism fits best?

options.A: A single global paragraph in the root CLAUDE.md that lists every payments and resolver path convention together in one block so all the rules stay permanently present in the session context
options.B: Separate `.claude/rules/` files whose glob frontmatter (`services/payments/**`, `**/*.resolver.ts`) auto-applies each rule by matching file path
options.C: PreToolUse hooks that scan each proposed edit and reject the call whenever the changed file does not literally contain the token 'Money'
options.D: Personal `~/.claude/commands/` entries that each developer remembers to trigger by hand whenever they start editing files under those paths

explanation: Correct: `.claude/rules/` files use glob frontmatter to attach path-scoped conventions automatically based on the file being worked on, regardless of its directory depth. A is wrong: a flat global CLAUDE.md paragraph applies everywhere and does not scope conventions to matching paths, making it noisy and imprecise. C is wrong: a keyword-matching hook is a blunt, error-prone gate, not a convention-injection mechanism, and would block legitimate code. D is wrong: personal slash commands are manual, per-user, and unshared — not automatic path-based conventions.

q: A CI job runs Claude Code to auto-generate a changelog from merged commits and write it to a file. A developer copies their interactive habits into the pipeline and the job hangs forever waiting for input. A teammate claims the fix is to set `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=1` and pass `--batch`. What is actually correct?

options.A: Set the `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=1` environment variable, the documented way to suppress the interactive prompt when Claude Code runs inside a CI pipeline
options.B: Pass the `--batch` flag so the pipeline can queue several prompts in sequence and run them without ever attaching to a TTY
options.C: Run `claude -p "..."` (equivalently `--print`), the non-interactive headless mode that takes the prompt as an argument and exits
options.D: Pipe `yes |` into the interactive `claude` session so that every single permission prompt the running job encounters is automatically confirmed for you

explanation: Correct: `claude -p` (alias `--print`) is the non-interactive/headless invocation for CI — it takes the prompt and runs to completion without an interactive session. A is wrong: there is no `CLAUDE_HEADLESS` environment variable; it is a classic distractor. B is wrong: there is no `--batch` flag. D is wrong: auto-confirming an interactive session with `yes` is a fragile hack and not how headless mode is meant to be run; it also doesn't supply the missing `-p` prompt argument.

q: During a session, Claude asks to run `terraform apply` against production infrastructure and the developer declines the permission prompt. Moments later Claude proposes the exact same `terraform apply` again, reasoning that maybe the user will accept this time. What is the correct interpretation of the permission/allowlist model?

options.A: A denial means the user refused; Claude should treat it as 'no,' adjust its approach, and not blindly retry the identical call
options.B: A denial is usually a transient prompt glitch, so simply re-issuing the same `terraform apply` command unchanged is the recommended recovery path to take here
options.C: Once any earlier command in the session was approved, every later command including this `terraform apply` is permanently allowed without prompting again
options.D: Permission prompts exist only in the IDE integration, so when running in the plain CLI the `terraform apply` would simply have executed regardless

explanation: Correct: a denied tool call means the user refused; the right behavior is to adjust the approach rather than re-issue the identical call hoping for a different answer. B is wrong: a denial is an intentional refusal, not a glitch, and blind retries are exactly what to avoid. C is wrong: approval is per-context, not a permanent blanket grant for all future commands. D is wrong: the permissions/approval model also applies to the CLI, not only the IDE.

q: A team shares a project `.claude/settings.json` (committed) that defines hooks and an allowlist everyone should inherit. One developer wants to add a permission for a local-only experimental script and an env var pointing at their personal sandbox, without forcing those onto teammates or committing them. Where should that go?

options.A: Append both the permission and the env var to the committed `.claude/settings.json` so the configuration stays identical for the whole team
options.B: Put them in `.claude/settings.local.json`, which holds personal machine-local overrides and is not committed or shared with teammates
options.C: Add them as a note in the root CLAUDE.md describing the permission and env var so the harness picks them up and applies them
options.D: Create a new `~/.claude/commands/` entry that exports the env var and grants the permission whenever that developer chooses to invoke it

explanation: Correct: `settings.local.json` is for personal, machine-local settings that should not be committed or imposed on teammates, layered over the shared `settings.json`. A is wrong: editing the committed `settings.json` would push the personal experiment onto everyone. C is wrong: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded context/instructions, not a settings/permissions/env-var configuration file. D is wrong: a personal slash command is a manual action, not a place to declare standing permissions or environment configuration.

q: An architect is asked to migrate a 40-table SQLAlchemy schema to a new multi-tenant design touching dozens of models, migrations, and query layers, where several valid sequencing strategies exist and a wrong order risks data-integrity bugs. The architect wants Claude to lay out the approach for review before any file is changed. Which Claude Code capability is the best fit?

options.A: Plan mode, which produces a structured plan for a large multi-file task with multiple valid approaches before any edit is made
options.B: A PostToolUse hook that automatically documents and records each schema change after the corresponding migration file has already been written to disk
options.C: Headless `claude -p` invoked once so the entire 40-table multi-tenant migration, including every model and query-layer rewrite, runs unattended end to end in a single pass
options.D: A `.claude/rules/` glob that auto-applies the migration's naming and typing conventions to each model file as it is edited

explanation: Correct: plan mode is designed for complex, large-scale, architectural tasks with multiple valid approaches — it lets Claude present a plan for review before editing. B is wrong: a PostToolUse hook reacts after changes are made, which is the opposite of planning before editing. C is wrong: running such a risky multi-strategy migration unattended in headless mode removes the human review the architect explicitly wants. D is wrong: rules inject per-file conventions but do not produce an up-front migration strategy for review.

q: A team lead wants Claude to break a large feature into independent pieces and have a fresh, isolated agent investigate each one (codebase exploration, dependency analysis) in parallel, returning summaries without polluting the main conversation's context. Which Claude Code feature is intended for this?

options.A: Subagents launched via the Task tool, which run independent investigations in isolation and report summaries back to the main agent
options.B: Multiple CLAUDE.md files, one written per feature piece, all loaded into context simultaneously so each area is documented
options.C: A PreToolUse hook configured to fork the current conversation into a separate parallel investigation thread every single time the main agent issues any tool call
options.D: settings.local.json profiles that each spawn their own separate chat window so the work can be split across parallel sessions

explanation: Correct: subagents (via the Task tool) are made for delegating self-contained investigation/work and returning summaries, keeping the main context clean. B is wrong: CLAUDE.md files are static context, not parallel investigating agents. C is wrong: PreToolUse hooks intercept tool calls deterministically; they do not fork conversations into investigations. D is wrong: there is no 'profiles spawn chat windows' feature in settings.local.json; that file holds local config overrides.

q: A team wants Claude Code to query their internal observability platform (logs, traces) and their issue tracker directly while debugging. They want these external tools exposed to Claude in a standard way that several teammates can share. What is the appropriate configuration?

options.A: Configure MCP servers for the observability platform and issue tracker so Claude calls those tools through the Model Context Protocol
options.B: Paste representative API responses from both systems into CLAUDE.md so the relevant data is always present in the session context
options.C: Write a PostToolUse hook that scrapes the observability dashboards and the issue tracker's web UI automatically after every single file edit in the session completes
options.D: Add both integrations as `~/.claude/commands/` entries so each developer manually pulls the logs and issues whenever they need them

explanation: Correct: MCP (Model Context Protocol) server configuration is the standard way to expose external tools/data sources (like observability and issue trackers) to Claude. B is wrong: CLAUDE.md is static auto-loaded context, not a live data feed, and pasting responses gets stale immediately. C is wrong: a PostToolUse hook runs deterministic automation after tools; it is not a way to provide queryable external tooling. D is wrong: personal slash commands are manual shortcuts, not a shared, standardized external-tool integration.

q: A frontend guild packages reusable, multi-step expertise — how to scaffold a new component with their design-system tokens, tests, and Storybook story following house conventions — so Claude can invoke that capability on demand with its bundled instructions and helper files. Which Claude Code mechanism is purpose-built for packaging such reusable capabilities?

options.A: A skill, which bundles instructions and supporting resources for a reusable capability Claude invokes on demand when it is relevant
options.B: A line in the root CLAUDE.md instructing Claude to 'know how to scaffold components with our design-system tokens and tests'
options.C: A PreToolUse hook that intercepts and blocks any edit to component or Storybook story files unless the full scaffolding sequence has demonstrably already been run first
options.D: settings.json permission entries that allowlist the scaffolding shell command so Claude is authorized to execute it directly

explanation: Correct: skills package reusable, multi-step capabilities with their own instructions and bundled resources that Claude can invoke when relevant — exactly this scaffolding workflow. B is wrong: a vague CLAUDE.md sentence is unstructured context, not a packaged, invokable capability with bundled files. C is wrong: a PreToolUse hook is a deterministic gate/automation, not a way to package expertise. D is wrong: an allowlist permission merely authorizes a command to run; it carries none of the procedural knowledge or resources a skill bundles.

q: A security-conscious team configures a PreToolUse hook in settings.json that inspects proposed Bash commands and blocks any matching `rm -rf` or writes outside the repo, returning an error to Claude. A developer assumes that because Claude 'decides' what to run, a hook can't actually stop a tool call. What is correct about hooks and the permission flow?

options.A: PreToolUse hooks run in the harness before the tool executes and can deny the call, deterministically blocking dangerous commands whatever the model intended to run
options.B: Hooks can only ever fire after a given tool has already finished running, so they are able to log a dangerous command afterward for later human review but can never actually prevent it
options.C: Hooks merely surface advisory text alongside the proposed command, which the model is then free to read, weigh, and ultimately ignore
options.D: Only the IDE integration is able to block tool calls; hooks declared in settings.json carry no real enforcement power when running in the CLI

explanation: Correct: PreToolUse hooks execute in the harness before a tool runs and can intercept and deny the call, giving deterministic enforcement independent of the model's intent. B is wrong: that describes PostToolUse; PreToolUse runs before execution and can prevent it. C is wrong: hooks are harness-executed automation with real enforcement, not advisory text the model may ignore. D is wrong: settings.json hooks enforce in the CLI as well, not only in an IDE.
